back_translation: When the enemies of Judah and Benjamin heard that the returned exiles were building a temple for the LORD, the God of Israel,

they came to Zerubbabel and the heads of the families and said, “Let us build with you, for we seek your God as you do, and we have been sacrificing to him since the days of Esarhaddon king of Assyria who brought us here.”

But Zerubbabel, Jeshua, and the rest of the heads of the families of Israel answered, “You have no part with us in building a house for our God, for we alone will build for the LORD, the God of Israel, as Cyrus king of Persia commanded us.”
Then the people of the land discouraged the people of Judah and made them afraid to build,
and they hired counselors to frustrate their plans throughout the reign of Cyrus king of Persia until the reign of Darius king of Persia.
In the reign of Ahasuerus, at the beginning of his reign, they wrote an accusation against the people of Judah and Jerusalem.

And in the days of Artaxerxes, Bishlam, Mithredath, Tabeel, and the rest of his associates wrote a letter to Artaxerxes king of Persia; the letter was written in Aramaic script and composed in Aramaic.
Rehum the commander and Shimshai the scribe wrote a letter against Jerusalem to King Artaxerxes as follows:

Rehum the commander, Shimshai the scribe, and the rest of their associates—the judges, officials, and administrators, together with the Persians, the Erechites, the Babylonians, and the Susaites (that is, the Elamites),
and the rest of the peoples whom the great and honorable Osnappar deported and settled in the city of Samaria and elsewhere west of the Euphrates.
(This is a copy of the letter they sent to him.) To King Artaxerxes, from your servants, the men of the region west of the Euphrates:

Let it be known to the king that the Jews who came up from you to us have arrived at Jerusalem and are rebuilding that rebellious and wicked city; they are repairing its walls and laying its foundations anew.
Now let it be known to the king that if this city is rebuilt and its walls are finished, they will no longer pay tribute, duty, or toll, and the royal treasury will suffer.
Now because we are maintained by the palace and it is not fitting for us to watch the king be dishonored, we have therefore sent to inform the king,

so that a search may be made in the book of records of your fathers; and in those records you will find and learn that this city is a rebellious city, harmful to kings and provinces, a center of sedition from ancient times—which is why this city was destroyed.
We inform the king that if this city is rebuilt and its walls are finished, you will have no dominion in the region west of the Euphrates.
The king sent a reply to Rehum the commander, Shimshai the scribe, and the rest of their associates living in Samaria and elsewhere in the region west of the Euphrates: “Greetings.
The letter you sent us has been read in translation before me.
I issued a decree, and a search was made, and it was found that this city has revolted against kings from ancient times, a place of rebellion and sedition.
Moreover, mighty kings have ruled over Jerusalem and governed the whole region west of the Euphrates, and tribute, duty, and toll were paid to them.
Now therefore issue an order for these men to stop, so that this city will not be rebuilt until I issue a command.
Be careful not to neglect this matter. Why should this threat grow to the harm of the royal interests?”
When the copy of King Artaxerxes' letter was read to Rehum, Shimshai the scribe, and their associates, they went quickly to the Jews in Jerusalem and by force compelled them to stop.
So the work on the house of God in Jerusalem ceased, and it remained at a standstill until the second year of the reign of Darius king of Persia.
